name: Family
description: Stories structured around family relationships and generational dynamics, often blending warmth, conflict, and reconciliation.

nodes.label: Family Genre Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a story in which family relationships are the central dramatic engine — where belonging, love, and the bonds between generations create both conflict and resolution, and where emotional stakes are rooted in the need to be known, accepted, and connected by those who share your blood or chosen family.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as a family story
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
typical_variants: Animated family adventure
Multigenerational drama
Family comedy
Coming-of-age within family context
failure_modes: Family is backdrop rather than the story's engine
Emotional stakes are external rather than relational
signals_in_text: Family relationships are introduced early and established as the source of central conflict
Tone invites emotional engagement across age groups

nodes.label: Family Relationships Must Be the Primary Story Engine
nodes.definition: The driving conflict, central stakes, and resolution arc must all flow from family relationships — not from external plot events that merely involve family members. An adventure is a family story only if the adventure tests, threatens, or ultimately deepens the family bond. A drama is a family story only if the rift between family members IS the drama.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Every major plot turn has a direct consequence for a family relationship
typical_variants: Estrangement and reunion
Generational inheritance of values or wounds
Sibling rivalry resolved through shared crisis
Parent-child renegotiation of roles
failure_modes: External plot (quest, crime, adventure) overshadows relational stakes
Family members are incidental to a story that could happen to anyone
signals_in_text: Scene outcomes change the state of at least one family relationship
Characters' decisions are motivated by their roles within the family

nodes.label: Content Must Be Accessible Across Age Groups
nodes.definition: Family stories must function for multiple age groups simultaneously — children must find engagement (adventure, humor, wonder, relatable stakes) while adults find emotional depth, thematic resonance, and honest treatment of conflict. This does not mean sanitizing reality; it means framing darkness in ways that are not traumatizing for young audiences while remaining honest for adult ones.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Story can be experienced meaningfully by both a child and an adult in the same audience
typical_variants: Layered subtext (children read surface, adults read depth)
Child protagonist with adult emotional context operating in parallel
Tone that is warm but not dishonest about difficulty
failure_modes: Content is exclusively aimed at children with nothing for adults
Content contains elements inappropriate or traumatic for children
Condescension toward either age group
signals_in_text: Dialogue and stakes work on at least two registers simultaneously
Humor is not exclusively childish nor exclusively adult
Emotional truth is present without graphic or age-inappropriate content

nodes.label: Emotional Stakes Are Rooted in Belonging and Love
nodes.definition: The emotional core of a family story must be the need to belong, to be loved, to be seen by one's family, or the fear of losing that connection. External stakes (safety, money, adventure outcomes) are meaningful only insofar as they threaten or illuminate these relational stakes. A character risks their life in a family story not primarily for survival, but because the family they love is at stake.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Audience can identify what each character most needs from their family and what they fear losing
typical_variants: Fear of parental disappointment
Desire to be understood across a generational gap
Grief over a lost family member shaping present relationships
Need to belong to a family one was separated from or never had
failure_modes: Emotional stakes are purely external — survival or material gain without relational dimension
Characters do not visibly need anything from each other
signals_in_text: Characters' deepest fears and hopes are tied to family acceptance or loss
The worst imaginable outcome for the protagonist involves family rupture, not just physical harm

nodes.label: Resolution Trends Toward Connection or Understanding
nodes.definition: Family stories generally resolve toward restored or newly forged connection — estrangements are healed, misunderstandings are cleared, generational gaps are bridged, or a family reconstituted after loss. Resolution need not be perfectly happy, but must represent meaningful movement toward understanding, acceptance, or belonging. Tragic endings are possible but must honor the emotional weight of the relational stakes.
entry_conditions: Family conflict and belonging stakes established
exit_conditions: Ending represents genuine movement toward connection, understanding, or earned peace between family members
typical_variants: Full reconciliation with restored relationship
Partial understanding that opens a door previously closed
Grief accepted and family reconstituted around absence
Coming-of-age departure honored by the family's blessing
failure_modes: Resolution is purely plot-level with no relational change
Family relationships end in the same state they began — no movement
Resolution is achieved through a plot device rather than earned emotional work
signals_in_text: Final scenes show a tangible shift in how family members relate to each other
The emotional need identified at the story's start is addressed at the end

nodes.label: Animated Family Adventure
nodes.definition: A family story in which an external adventure (quest, journey, threat, discovery) serves as the vehicle through which family bonds are tested and deepened. The animation or visual spectacle carries young audiences while the relational arc carries adults. The adventure must be legible as a metaphor for the family's internal journey.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
External adventure is the surface narrative
Protagonist is typically a child or young character
exit_conditions: Adventure plot and family relationship arc resolve in the same climactic moment
typical_variants: Quest to save a family member
Journey that forces parent and child to see each other differently
Coming-of-age adventure that requires leaving and returning to family
Found-family adventure among misfits
failure_modes: Adventure is self-contained with family as mere backdrop
Relational arc is resolved in a single line of dialogue after the adventure is over
signals_in_text: The adventure's climax requires the protagonist to make a choice rooted in their family relationship
Visual spectacle is paired with emotional beats throughout, not just at the end

nodes.label: Family Drama / Multigenerational
nodes.definition: A realist or dramatic family story in which the conflict spans multiple generations — patterns, wounds, secrets, or traditions that pass down through a family shape the present narrative. The drama lives in the gap between who family members wish they were to each other and who they actually are.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
At least two generations are active in the narrative
Emotional realism is the primary mode
exit_conditions: Generational patterns are made visible and at least partially addressed by the resolution
typical_variants: Family secret revealed across generations
Inheritance dispute as proxy for unspoken grief
Child refusing to repeat a parent's mistakes
Deathbed or reunion that forces long-avoided conversations
failure_modes: Generational conflict is merely backdrop for individual character drama
Drama is unrelentingly bleak with no movement toward understanding
signals_in_text: Characters explicitly or implicitly echo or invert their parents' choices
Scenes in the past or references to family history illuminate present conflict

nodes.label: Family Comedy
nodes.definition: A comedy in which the humor is generated by family dynamics — the specific absurdities of people who know each other too well, love each other despite themselves, and cannot escape their shared history. The humor must be warm enough to signal safety across age groups, and conflict must resolve with genuine affection.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Humor is the primary audience experience
Conflict is generated by family dynamics rather than external threat
exit_conditions: Comic situations arise from the specific characters' family relationships, not generic comedic scenarios
typical_variants: Holiday gathering chaos
Blended family adjustment comedy
Sibling rivalry escalation
Parent-child role reversal
failure_modes: Humor is mean-spirited — family members demean each other without genuine affection underneath
Comedy is disconnected from family relationships and could happen to any group
signals_in_text: Laughs arise from character-specific family dynamics
Even at peak comic conflict, affection is legible beneath the friction

nodes.label: Coming-of-Age Within Family Context
nodes.definition: A story in which a young protagonist's growth into greater independence, identity, or maturity is structured entirely through their relationship with family. The family is not just the world the protagonist must leave — it is the force shaping who they become, and the reconciliation of the self they are becoming with the family they came from is the emotional climax.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Protagonist is a young person negotiating identity and independence
Family relationship is the primary arena of that negotiation
exit_conditions: Protagonist achieves a new understanding of themselves that also transforms their relationship with their family
typical_variants: Adolescent refusing a parent's path who eventually finds their own way with family's blessing
Young person discovering a family secret that reframes their identity
First major departure from home — physical or emotional — and what it costs and gives
failure_modes: Coming-of-age arc is purely external — protagonist grows through adventure with family as passive observers
Family is purely antagonistic with no genuine love underneath the conflict
signals_in_text: Protagonist's internal growth is mirrored in a changed family dynamic at the resolution
The specific history and texture of this family shapes the protagonist's particular path to maturity

nodes.label: Home and Family Spaces Are Emotionally Charged
nodes.definition: In family stories, domestic spaces — the family home, the kitchen table, the childhood bedroom, the family car — carry emotional history and serve as active participants in the drama. A scene in the family home is never just a scene in a house; the space embodies the relationships, memories, and wounds of the people who inhabit it.
entry_conditions: Subgenre pattern selected
exit_conditions: Physical domestic spaces are rendered with emotional specificity that amplifies the relational drama
typical_variants: The family home that must be sold representing dissolution of family identity
The dinner table where everything is performed and nothing is said
The childhood bedroom a returning adult no longer fits
A family road trip vehicle as inescapable shared space
failure_modes: Domestic settings are generic and interchangeable — no emotional history is embedded in the space
Important family confrontations happen in neutral or public spaces that dilute the intimacy
signals_in_text: Description of domestic spaces includes emotional or memory-laden details
Characters' behavior in family spaces reflects their relationship to their family history

nodes.label: Family Rituals and Traditions Structure the Narrative
nodes.definition: Family stories are often organized around rituals — holidays, meals, weddings, funerals, annual gatherings — that concentrate family members in shared space and make avoided conversations unavoidable. These rituals carry the weight of past iterations and are the narrative occasions through which family dynamics are performed, tested, and transformed.
entry_conditions: Family relationships and domestic setting established
exit_conditions: At least one family ritual or tradition serves as a structuring event for the narrative
typical_variants: Holiday gathering that forces estranged family members together
Wedding that reorders family loyalties and alliances
Funeral that unlocks secrets and prompts renegotiation of family roles
Annual tradition that has calcified and must be broken or transformed
failure_modes: Story has no organizing family events — characters interact without the pressure of shared ritual
Ritual is present but purely as backdrop with no emotional function
signals_in_text: Anticipation or dread of the family event is established before it occurs
The ritual's outcome differs from its normal performance, marking a turning point in family dynamics

nodes.label: Multiple Generations or Age Groups Interact
nodes.definition: Family stories require the active presence of multiple generations or age groups — grandparents, parents, children, or any configuration spanning different life stages — because the friction and depth of family drama emerges from the gap between different life stages, different historical contexts, and different understandings of what the family is and should be.
entry_conditions: Family relationships established as story engine
exit_conditions: At least two distinct generations or age groups are active characters with agency in the narrative
typical_variants: Three-generation household where values conflict
Young adult returning home and encountering parents as people rather than roles
Grandparent and grandchild bond that bridges or reveals parental gap
Sibling group navigating inherited family roles
failure_modes: All characters are the same generation — family story becomes a peer relationship story
Older or younger generation is present but passive — merely witnessed rather than active in the drama
signals_in_text: Characters of different generations hold meaningfully different perspectives on the family's history and future
Scenes explicitly show the gap between what one generation assumes and what another experiences

nodes.label: Family Gathering / Disruption Scene
nodes.definition: A scene in which the family is brought together — at a meal, a holiday, a ritual occasion, or simply in shared domestic space — and the gathering is disrupted: by an announcement, an arrival, a confrontation, or an unwanted truth. This scene establishes the family's surface dynamic and introduces the fracture that will drive the story.
entry_conditions: Family relationships and domestic setting established
exit_conditions: The family has been shown in nominal togetherness, and a disruption has set the central conflict in motion
typical_variants: Dinner table confrontation that breaks a long silence
Holiday gathering upended by unexpected arrival or announcement
Family meeting called to discuss a crisis that reveals deeper rifts
Ordinary domestic scene interrupted by news that changes everything
failure_modes: Gathering is peaceful and undisrupted — no friction established
Disruption is external (a disaster) with no internal family dimension
Scene shows only one family member's perspective without revealing the group dynamic
signals_in_text: Multiple family members are present and their relationships to each other are established in a short span
The disruption's nature reveals something about the family that was previously unspoken or suppressed

nodes.label: Generational Clash or Misunderstanding Scene
nodes.definition: A scene in which the gap between generations becomes the explicit site of conflict — where a parent and child, or grandparent and grandchild, or siblings at different life stages, fail to understand each other in a way that illuminates both the love and the limitation in their relationship. This scene must carry genuine empathy for both sides of the generational divide.
entry_conditions: Family disruption established
Multiple generations active in the narrative
exit_conditions: Audience understands why both parties feel what they feel, even if the characters cannot yet understand each other
typical_variants: Parent refusing to see a child as an adult
Child unable to recognize the sacrifice behind a parent's control
Grandparent's life wisdom dismissed by grandchild who must discover it independently
Sibling who stayed versus sibling who left — different experiences of the same family
failure_modes: One generation is clearly right and the other clearly wrong — no genuine empathy for both sides
Clash is resolved too quickly before its emotional weight is fully felt
Misunderstanding is trivial rather than rooted in genuine difference of experience
signals_in_text: Scene shows the interiority or emotional logic of both parties even when only one is present
The clash is rooted in love or care, not mere antagonism
Audience feels the tragedy of two people who love each other failing to connect

nodes.label: Reconciliation / Reunion Scene
nodes.definition: A scene in which estranged or conflicted family members achieve meaningful connection — a breakthrough conversation, a gesture of understanding, a shared grief, or a reunion after separation. This scene must be earned by the emotional work done in prior scenes; the reconciliation is not a reset but a transformation of the relationship.
entry_conditions: Generational clash or family rupture has been fully dramatized
Characters have undergone sufficient change or revelation to make reconciliation possible
exit_conditions: Family relationship is in a meaningfully different state than before — connection is real, not performed
Resolution honors the difficulty of what was overcome
typical_variants: Explicit conversation that names what was previously unspoken
Wordless gesture or action that communicates acceptance
Reunion after physical or emotional separation that shows both parties have changed
Shared experience of grief or joy that dissolves prior distance
failure_modes: Reconciliation is stated rather than shown — characters declare they've made up without dramatizing it
Reconciliation undoes the conflict entirely, as if it never happened, rather than transforming the relationship
Scene is too short relative to the weight of the preceding conflict
signals_in_text: The specific wound or misunderstanding driving the conflict is addressed, not talked around
Characters' behavior toward each other after reconciliation is visibly different from before
Emotional release (tears, laughter, silence) is earned rather than manufactured

nodes.label: Warmth, Emotional Accessibility, and Earned Sentiment
nodes.definition: Family stories require a tone of genuine warmth — not enforced cheerfulness, but the quality of caring about these characters and their relationships with visible affection. The tone must make emotional moments accessible rather than defended against, inviting audiences to feel rather than merely observe. Sentiment is permissible and powerful when it is earned by honest conflict and genuine character work.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Emotional moments land with genuine feeling rather than manufactured sentiment or ironic deflection
typical_variants: Warm and generous narrative voice that loves its characters
Tonal balance that earns big emotional moments through prior restraint
Humor used to release tension before returning to emotional depth
Gentleness in representing childhood perspective alongside honesty about adult complexity
failure_modes: Ironic or detached tone prevents emotional access
Warmth is asserted rather than created — scenes tell us to feel without generating the feeling
Tone is relentlessly heavy — no warmth or humor to breathe between emotional beats
signals_in_text: Emotional scenes are given space and pace
Characters are rendered with affection even at their worst moments
Humor and warmth are present in the fabric of the story, not reserved only for the resolution

nodes.label: Saccharine Without Genuine Conflict
nodes.definition: Family stories fail when warmth and resolution are asserted without genuine conflict earning them — when families are presented as problems to be quickly solved rather than relationships to be honestly inhabited. Saccharine storytelling avoids the real difficulties of family life (the resentment, the silence, the unspoken grief, the way love can coexist with disappointment) in favor of surface harmony that rings false and leaves audiences unmoved.
entry_conditions: Story presents family conflict that resolves too easily or is not genuinely dramatized
exit_conditions: Conflict must be genuinely present and emotionally costly before resolution is permitted
typical_variants: Conflict resolved with a single conversation that characters accept without resistance
Antagonist family member who has a sudden change of heart without dramatized internal struggle
Emotional moments that arrive before the story has earned them
Resolution that ignores prior conflict rather than genuinely addressing it
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Audience feels manipulated into emotion rather than genuinely moved
The difficulty of the conflict is understated relative to the warmth of the resolution
Characters accept resolution too readily — no residue of prior hurt or misunderstanding

edges.label: Promise requires family relationships as primary engine
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Every plot event must have a direct consequence for a family relationship
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' motivations and decisions must flow from their family roles and bonds
common_alternatives: Family as context for a genre story that has its own primary engine (adventure, thriller)
anti_patterns: External plot drives the story with family as incidental backdrop

edges.label: Promise requires cross-generational accessibility
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes must be legible to both child and adult audience members
edges.effects_on_character: Characters of different ages must each carry narrative weight that their age group can identify with
common_alternatives: Stories may lean slightly toward one age group while still maintaining accessibility for the other
anti_patterns: Content exclusive to one age group — either purely juvenile or adult-only in theme and tone

edges.label: Promise requires belonging and love as emotional stakes
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: External dangers must translate into relational stakes — what does this mean for the family bond
edges.effects_on_character: Each character's deepest need must be connected to their place in the family
common_alternatives: Stakes may include external threats, but these are secondary to relational stakes
anti_patterns: Characters fight for survival or material goals with no relational dimension to the stakes

edges.label: Promise requires resolution trending toward connection
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience trusts that the family will move toward understanding even through genuine conflict
edges.effects_on_character: Characters will change through the story in ways that allow connection to be possible
common_alternatives: Bittersweet resolution that achieves understanding even without full reconciliation
anti_patterns: Resolution leaves family more fractured than they began — no movement toward connection

edges.label: Promise requires warmth and emotional accessibility
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone signals that characters' struggles are held with genuine care
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are rendered with affection even during their worst behavior
common_alternatives: Tone may be comedic, dramatic, or adventurous, but warmth must underlie all variants
anti_patterns: Cynical or coldly ironic tone that holds the family story at arm's length

edges.label: Family engine expressed through external adventure metaphor
preconditions: Family relationship is the engine AND an external adventure is the vehicle
edges.effects_on_stakes: Adventure stakes and relational stakes must resolve simultaneously
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's adventure arc and family relationship arc are inseparable
common_alternatives: Multigenerational drama
Family comedy
Coming-of-age
anti_patterns: Adventure is primary and family relationship is secondary or decorative

edges.label: Family engine expressed through generational pattern drama
preconditions: Family relationship is the engine AND multiple generations carry the dramatic weight
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes include the fate of inherited patterns — will they be continued or broken
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are defined by their position in a generational line
common_alternatives: Animated adventure
Family comedy
Coming-of-age
anti_patterns: Drama is set in a family context but generational dynamics are not active in the conflict

edges.label: Family engine expressed through comedic family dynamics
preconditions: Family relationship is the engine AND humor is the primary audience experience
edges.effects_on_stakes: Comic stakes arise from family dynamics — embarrassment, loyalty, affection misexpressed
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' comic behavior is rooted in their specific family roles and history
common_alternatives: Animated adventure
Multigenerational drama
Coming-of-age
anti_patterns: Humor is disconnected from family dynamics — any group of people could generate the same comedy

edges.label: Family engine expressed through protagonist's maturation within family
preconditions: Family relationship is the engine AND protagonist's growth is the central arc
edges.effects_on_stakes: Growing up means renegotiating one's place in the family — this is the central dramatic question
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's identity formation is inseparable from their family story
common_alternatives: Animated adventure
Multigenerational drama
Family comedy
anti_patterns: Coming-of-age is purely external — protagonist grows through peer relationships or adventure without family as the primary arena

edges.label: Animated adventure roots its emotional stakes in family home spaces
preconditions: Animated adventure subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Home is what is protected or left behind — its emotional weight anchors the external adventure
edges.effects_on_character: Characters carry the emotional memory of home into the adventure
common_alternatives: Found family on the road creates new home spaces
Adventure through memory of home
anti_patterns: Home is generic — adventure begins in a setting with no emotional weight

edges.label: Multigenerational drama is organized by family rituals
preconditions: Multigenerational drama subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Rituals concentrate family members and make avoidance impossible
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' behavior within rituals reveals who they are to each other across generations
common_alternatives: Crisis event (illness, death) as organizing structure rather than planned ritual
anti_patterns: Drama unfolds without organizing family occasions — characters interact without ritual pressure

edges.label: Family comedy requires multiple generations generating friction
preconditions: Family comedy subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Comic friction emerges from generational misunderstanding and differing expectations
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' comic behavior is rooted in their generational perspective and family role
common_alternatives: Sibling comedy (same generation) as primary comic engine
anti_patterns: Comedy is generated by a single generation without family dynamic friction

edges.label: Coming-of-age arc is anchored in family home as both sanctuary and constraint
preconditions: Coming-of-age subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Home is the space the protagonist must define themselves against and eventually leave or return to transformed
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's relationship to the family home tracks their internal development
common_alternatives: Boarding school or institutional setting that stands in for home
anti_patterns: Protagonist's coming-of-age occurs entirely outside family space with no return or reckoning

edges.label: Multigenerational drama requires active intergenerational conflict
preconditions: Multigenerational drama subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Each generation's version of the family story must be given narrative weight
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are shaped by the generation that preceded them and shape the generation that follows
common_alternatives: Focus on two generations with third implied through legacy
anti_patterns: Only one generation is dramatically active — others are passive observers or symbols

edges.label: Emotionally charged home space is the site of family gathering and disruption
preconditions: Home space established with emotional history
edges.effects_on_stakes: Disruption in a charged domestic space carries the weight of everything that space represents
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' behavior in home spaces reveals their relationship to the family's history
common_alternatives: Disruption may occur in transit to or from home rather than within it
anti_patterns: Gathering and disruption occur in a neutral space with no emotional dimension

edges.label: Family ritual is the occasion for gathering that is disrupted
preconditions: Family ritual established as narrative structure
edges.effects_on_stakes: Disruption of a ritual carries symbolic weight beyond the immediate event
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' relationship to the ritual reveals their relationship to the family identity
common_alternatives: Informal gathering disrupted rather than formal ritual
anti_patterns: Ritual occurs without disruption — family performs it without the fracture that makes it dramatically alive

edges.label: Intergenerational presence creates conditions for clash or misunderstanding
preconditions: Multiple generations active in the narrative
edges.effects_on_stakes: The clash reveals what each generation needs from the other and cannot ask for directly
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are forced to confront each other as people rather than roles
common_alternatives: Misunderstanding may be comic (family comedy) or tragic (drama) depending on subgenre
anti_patterns: Generations interact without genuine friction — they understand each other too easily

edges.label: Gathering disruption escalates into generational confrontation
preconditions: Gathering has been disrupted and tensions have surfaced
edges.effects_on_stakes: The generational clash makes explicit what the disruption made implicit
edges.effects_on_character: Characters who have been performing their family roles must now speak or act from their actual selves
common_alternatives: Clash may be deferred across multiple scenes rather than immediately following the disruption
anti_patterns: Disruption is resolved before it can escalate into genuine generational confrontation

edges.label: Generational clash, fully dramatized, creates conditions for reconciliation
preconditions: Generational clash has been fully dramatized with genuine empathy for both sides
Characters have had sufficient time and experience to change
edges.effects_on_stakes: Reconciliation is meaningful only because the difficulty of the clash is fully honored
edges.effects_on_character: Both parties must change or see each other differently — reconciliation is not one side capitulating
common_alternatives: Partial reconciliation that opens a door rather than fully healing the breach
anti_patterns: Reconciliation occurs before the clash is fully dramatized — resolution is cheap because conflict was not costly

edges.label: Belonging stakes are resolved through the reconciliation scene
preconditions: Belonging and love established as emotional stakes
Characters have been denied or threatened in their belonging
edges.effects_on_stakes: Reconciliation must address the specific belonging need that was established as the central stake
edges.effects_on_character: Characters receive or offer the recognition, acceptance, or love they needed
common_alternatives: Resolution may be partial — enough belonging restored to sustain the character rather than complete restoration
anti_patterns: Reconciliation resolves plot without addressing the belonging need — characters make up without the emotional wound being touched

edges.label: Resolution without genuine prior conflict becomes saccharine
preconditions: Resolution arrives without sufficient prior conflict, cost, or character change
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience does not feel the value of the resolution because they did not feel the weight of what was overcome
edges.effects_on_character: Characters arrive at connection without having genuinely struggled toward it
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid
